interrogator_dossier:
# Puig Brands, S.A. (PUIG.MC) — Forensic Earnings-Call Dossier

*Transcript span: H1-2024 (first call as public company, 2024-09-09) → Q1-2026 sales update (2026-05-01). Eight consecutive disclosures cross-referenced against the screener's multi-year financial trajectory. Signal type: deep_value.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Puig's story across eight quarters is, on the surface, one of the most internally consistent in the entire screener universe — and that consistency is itself the central forensic fact. From its IPO debut (2024-09-09) to its 2025 full-year print (2026-02-18), the core narrative never deviated: a family-controlled, founder-led "home of creativity" that owns 96% of its Love Brands, runs three of the top-10 global Prestige fragrance lines (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), and "outperforms the premium beauty market" via brand desirability, innovation cadence, and 75%+ gross margins. Management repeated the "outperform the premium beauty market" formulation in literally every call, and by Q1-2026 had codified it into a streak claim: *"as we have done consistently for each of the last 5 years, including each of the last 8 quarters as a public company"* (2026-05-01).

The arc has three distinct chapters:

**Chapter 1 — IPO confidence and the fragrance flywheel (H1-2024 → FY-2024).** The emphasis was offensive: Fragrance & Fashion (73% of revenue) compounding double-digit, Jean Paul Gaultier entering the top-10 for the first time, Good Girl becoming the #1 feminine line in the US. Growth *accelerated* through 2024 — H1 +8.5% LFL, 9M +9.6%, Q4 +14.1% LFL (FY +10.9% LFL) — capped by the strategically important extension of the Charlotte Tilbury partnership to 2031 with a path to full ownership. Makeup was the one soft spot (FY-2024 −1.3%), explained away as sell-in/sell-out destocking, the Christian Louboutin China drag, and the voluntary Airbrush Setting Spray withdrawal.

**Chapter 2 — Deceleration acknowledged, mix rotates (H1-2025 → Q3-2025).** The narrative shifted from "accelerating" to "moderating but resilient." Management proactively flagged fragrance-category deceleration: *"our best estimation of the growth of the category as a whole, it was in the mid-single-digit number… over the past couple of months, we're seeing moderation… we expect the second half to be more in the low single digit"* (2025-09-12). Critically, the growth *driver rotated* — Fragrance Q3-2025 decelerated to +2.8% LFL while Makeup exploded to +18.8% LFL (≈half attributed to a one-time Amazon US sell-in pipeline) and APAC surged +35.8% LFL (partly a subsidiary-consolidation artifact "we do not expect to repeat"). The honesty about these mechanical, non-repeatable boosts is a green flag for management candor.

**Chapter 3 — Succession executed, then the bombshell (FY-2025 → Q1-2026).** FY-2025 (2026-02-18) closed the IPO-vintage story cleanly: €5.04B revenue (the €5B milestone), +7.8% LFL (top of the 6–8% range), EBITDA margin expanded to 20.7%, leverage a trivial 0.7x. Then the arc snaps. The planned April-2026 Capital Markets Day — at which a new multi-year vision plan (successor to the 2021–2025 plan) was to be unveiled — was *postponed*, and on the Q1-2026 call (2026-05-01) a **new CEO, Jose Manuel Muniesa**, delivered his first call and confirmed *"ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with The Estée Lauder, in which the 2 companies will potentially merge their business"* (announced 2026-03-23). The growth-compounder narrative has been overtaken, mid-stride, by a transformational M&A narrative.

The single most important arc observation: **the operational story quietly downshifted across 2025 (fragrance category moderation, Q1-2026 LFL +4.7% — the slowest print in the dataset) at exactly the moment a strategic/M&A catalyst appeared.** The "deep_value" tag is anchored on the screener's 332% margin-of-safety to a €64 Buffett fair value against a €16 price — and that gap now has a live, dated forcing function attached to it.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The financials provided carry an important internal contradiction the director must understand before reading the table: the screener's fiscal-year block shows EPS rising 0.39→0.70→0.82→0.98→1.05 (2021–2025) and FY-2025 net income €594M, while the TTM/LATEST-QUARTER override block reports **TTM diluted EPS 1.99 and TTM FCF €1.28B** as of 2025-12-31. Both cannot describe the same share count on the same basis. Puig has a **dual-class structure** (Class A held by the family, Class B free-float from the IPO); the €1.04 "adjusted EPS" management quotes (2026-02-18) reconciles to the ~€594M net income on the *full* ~1.1B share base, whereas the 1.99 TTM diluted figure appears to be computed on the free-float B-share denominator. Per instruction the TTM figures are "current state," but for valuation the director must resolve which share count the €64 fair value assumes — this is the difference between a stock trading at ~8x and ~15x earnings. **This denominator ambiguity is the most material single uncertainty in the file and is not a management-credibility issue — it is a data-plumbing issue the screener must reconcile.**

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard-number check | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Outperform the premium beauty market" every period | All 8 calls | Revenue 2.59B→3.62B→4.30B→4.79B→5.04B; 3-yr CAGR +11.7%; FY-25 +7.8% LFL on a market they peg at low-to-mid-single-digit | **Corroborated.** Sustained share gains are real; the company genuinely grew well above a decelerating category. |

But note the recurring *Ulta-2023 → Amazon-2025* pattern: each distribution add front-loads a sell-in pump that later normalizes — a sell-in/sell-out treadmill the bears can model. |
| Fragrance category "will still grow at 6%, 7%" (IPO/market view) | 2024-10-29 | By 2025-09-12 management itself cut this read to "low single digit" for H2-25; Q1-26 fragrance +3.9% LFL | **Walked back.** The earliest market-growth framing did not hold; to their credit management revised it openly rather than defending it. |

**Net forensic read:** Where the numbers can be checked on a like-for-like basis, management is *corroborated or vindicated* on growth, FCF, leverage, and the makeup-timing call. The divergences are not credibility gaps — they are *measurement-basis* gaps (adjusted vs IFRS GM; dual-class EPS/Altman distortion). This is a high-integrity operating story with a noisy data overlay.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Overall: STABLE-to-mildly-declining on the operating cadence, with a hard regime-break at Q1-2026 (new voice, M&A overhang).**

- **H1-2024 (offensive, high confidence):** *"we are confident in the outlook"*; *"we continue to outperform the market."* Marc Puig sets a measured, founder's-long-view tone.
- **Q3-2024 (peak confidence):** *"retailers are being quite bullish on the fragrance category"*; an *acceleration* to +11.6% LFL. The most upbeat call in the set.
- **FY-2024 sales update (confident, milestone framing):** +14.1% Q4 LFL, CT extension to 2031 — celebratory but disciplined.
- **H1-2025 (first defensive notes):** *"some moderation in underlying growth, which was particularly evident in Q2"*; guidance nudged to *"the lower side of the range."* The pivot from offense to candor begins. Simultaneously, the **Deputy CEO appointment of Jose Manuel Albesa** is announced — the first visible succession signal.
- **Q3-2025 (recovered confidence, but narrower base):** Guidance *raised* back to the *middle* of the range on a healthy open-to-buy; *"September and October have gone high, and the mood we've seen in the open-to-buy has been healthy."* Confidence restored, but now carried disproportionately by Makeup/APAC one-offs rather than the fragrance core.
- **FY-2025 (steady, valedictory):** Top-of-range delivery, margin beat. Marc Puig's last full-results call reads like a clean handoff.
- **Q1-2026 (new CEO, guarded, M&A-shadowed):** Jose Manuel Muniesa opens by *addressing the Estée Lauder talks first*, then a deliberately reassuring operating tone — *"record Q1 sales and a solid start"* — over the slowest LFL in the dataset (+4.7%) and a new geopolitical drag (*"the ongoing situation in the Middle East had an estimated minus 1.2% impact"*). The CMD postponement is acknowledged with palpable regret.

The specificity of guidance *increased* over time (management volunteered category-growth estimates, market-share basis points, FX bps, Argentina hyperinflation euros to the million), which is a credibility positive — until Q1-2026, where the M&A confidentiality wall *necessarily* reduced forward color. The tonal decline is therefore **mostly exogenous** (succession + deal silence + Middle East) rather than a deterioration in the underlying business.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — **HIGH**

Puig is, on this dataset, a serial guidance-deliverer. The track record:

- IPO "high-single-digit LFL" medium-term guide → **delivered 10.9% LFL in FY-2024** (over-delivered).
- FY-2025 "6–8% LFL" guide, set at IPO and reaffirmed all year → tracked from "lower side" (Sept) to "middle" (Oct) to **"top end" actual +7.8%** (2026-02-18). The intra-year upgrades were *earned*, not walked back.
- FY-2025 "EBITDA margin expansion in line with 2024" → **delivered 20.7% vs 20.2%, "comfortably above guidance."**
- FY-2024 Makeup "gap will narrow" → **narrowed and reversed to +13.7% in 2025.**

This maps cleanly to the screener's **beat 2/3 quarters (67%)** EPS-beat rate and the EPS trajectory (0.82→0.98→1.05). There is a mild *sandbagging* signature — guiding to the low end mid-year and finishing at the top end — which is the favorable kind of credibility pattern (under-promise/over-deliver). The one genuine walk-back was the *market-growth* assumption (6–7% → low-single-digit), but that is a category call, not a company guide, and it was revised transparently.

**Reliability: HIGH.** Eight-for-eight on "outperform the market"; consistent delivery against self-set ranges; mechanically honest about non-repeatable boosts. The only forward-guidance risk now is that the Estée Lauder process makes 2026 guidance *deliberately vague* — Q1-2026 offered only "outperform the market" + "EBITDA margins broadly stable," far thinner than the prior cadence.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The Q&A reveals a remarkably *informed, persistent* sell-side (Santander/Szachtman, CaixaBank/Rito, Barclays/Folan, GS/Nicolai, Alantra, BofA, JB Capital) circling the same five concerns:

1. **The sell-in/sell-out treadmill (the dominant recurring worry).** Analysts pressed *every quarter* on whether Makeup growth was real demand or pipeline fill — first Ulta-2023, then Amazon-2025. Folan (2025-10-30): *"how much of a benefit the Charlotte Tilbury sell-in on Amazon was."* Management conceded *"Amazon represents about half of that 18.8% growth"* — a candid admission, but it validated the analysts' instinct that the headline overstated underlying demand. Pannuti (2025-10-30) explicitly invoked the Ulta precedent to ask when normalization hits. **This is the bears' best-supported line and it recurs to the present (Colaco, 2026-05-01, on Q2 sell-in normalization).**

2. **Fragrance-category deceleration & market-share slippage.** Rito repeatedly probed share losses; FY-2025 confirmed VMS slipped 11.5%→11.1%, which management half-attributed to LatAm promotional intensity it "didn't necessarily follow" and half to "improved measurement." A real, if minor, ding to the share-gain narrative.

3. **"Why don't you move faster on Charlotte Tilbury?"** (Rito, 2025-10-30) — analysts see the distribution white-space and are frustrated by the deliberate, scarcity-protecting pace. Management's answer (protect desirability) is strategically sound but leaves visible upside un-captured, which the bull case can frame as latent optionality and the bear case as under-monetization.

4. **APAC quality-of-growth.** The +35.8% Q3 / +21.7% FY APAC prints drew scrutiny; management volunteered that a *subsidiary-consolidation uplift "we do not expect to repeat 2026"* inflated them — pre-empting the bad-news reveal honestly.

5. **Margin phasing / A&P intensity** (Folan, Szachtman) — the H1-2025 +140bps A&P spike (Million Gold US launch) compressed Fragrance segment margin 18.6%→17.8%; management guided it would not persist, and FY-2025 margin expansion proved that out.

**Topics analysts pushed on that *became* bad news:** the Makeup sell-in question (Amazon normalization now an explicit Q2-2026 headwind); the fragrance-category-growth question (downgraded to low-single-digit); LatAm promotional pressure (a recurring drag). **Topic management has NOT addressed and analysts will demand answers on:** the *terms, ratio, and rationale* of the Estée Lauder combination — a confidentiality black box that dominates the current information vacuum.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Eight-for-eight market outperformance, transparently evidenced** — VMS figures, top-10 rankings, country-by-country share data (Q1-2026: +1.4% Germany, +1.8% France, +1.1% Italy, +0.2% US in Makeup sell-out).
🟢 **FCF inflection** — TTM FCF €1.28B, +38% YoY, 3-yr CAGR +41.4%, 64% conversion; deleveraging to 0.7x. A cash machine, not a story stock.
🟢 **Management candor on non-repeatable boosts** — voluntarily flagged Amazon sell-in (~half of Makeup growth), APAC subsidiary-consolidation uplift, and Argentina hyperinflation swings to the euro. This is the behavior of a team that respects the audience.
🟢 **Guidance over-delivery with favorable sandbagging** — low-end → top-end pattern in FY-2025.
🟢 **Makeup recovery vindicated the "timing not demand" thesis** — a clean, checkable win.
🟢 **CT partnership locked to 2031 with path to 100% ownership** — secures the second growth engine.

🔴 **The sell-in/sell-out treadmill** — recurring distribution-launch pipeline fills (Ulta→Amazon→Boots) repeatedly flatter headline growth and create predictable normalization air-pockets; an explicit Q2-2026 risk.
🔴 **Fragrance core decelerating** — the 73%-of-revenue engine fell from +11–14% LFL (2024) to +3.9% LFL (Q1-2026); the growth base is rotating onto smaller, lumpier segments.
🔴 **Gross-margin contraction signal** — screener flags GM "contracting," −0.35σ vs peak; management's own GM ticked 75.8%→75.1% FY-on-H1.
🔴 **Market-share slippage** — VMS 11.5%→11.1%, partially conceded as genuine loss to promotional competitors.
🔴 **Succession + governance flux at the worst moment** — Deputy-CEO (mid-2025) → new CEO (by Q1-2026) → CMD postponed → vision plan shelved → M&A talks. A lot of organizational change layered onto a transformational deal.
🔴 **Estée Lauder overhang is double-edged** — it could be the re-rating forcing function *or* a deal-break/dilution risk; the confidentiality wall means investors are flying blind on terms. **Insider buy ratio 0.00** (no insider buying into the gap), and **Smart-money 26/100** — neither the family nor institutions are signaling conviction *through purchases*, though the family's control stake makes open-market buying unlikely regardless.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The voice change is the loudest signal.** The Q1-2026 call is the first not led by Marc Puig — Jose Manuel Muniesa (CEO) and Miquel Angel Serra (CFO) replace Marc Puig and Joan Albiol/Ramis. A founder-CEO stepping back from the earnings stage *immediately before/during a merger with a US strategic* is a textbook signal of a control-event in motion. The Deputy-CEO creation (2025-09-12) reads in hindsight as the first move of a deliberate succession choreography, not a routine org tweak.
- **"Potential business combination… merge their business"** (not "be acquired," not "sell") — the language implies a *merger of equals or stock-for-stock combination* with Estée Lauder, consistent with two family/founder-influenced houses combining rather than a clean cash takeout. This matters enormously for the deep-value thesis: a stock-merger re-rates Puig toward a *combined-entity* multiple and synergy NPV, but may not deliver the clean cash premium a 332% MOS implies.
- **CMD postponement = optionality preserved.** Shelving the multi-year vision plan *because of* the deal tells you the deal is advanced enough that standalone guidance would be moot — a tell that talks are substantive, not exploratory.
- **Terminology drift toward "normalization" and "discipline."** Across 2025 the lexicon shifted from "acceleration/outperformance/momentum" to "disciplined execution / cost discipline / resilience / more normalized market environment." Classic late-cycle defensive vocabulary creep, even as the absolute numbers held.
- **APAC reframed from afterthought to priority** — *"APAC represents over 1/3 of the global beauty market, yet accounts for just 11% of group sales… now is the right moment to accelerate"* (2026-05-01). The new CEO is planting a self-help growth flag — useful as a standalone re-rating lever if the EL deal *fails*.
- **Capital allocation discipline is unusually conservative for the sector** — 40% dividend payout, 2x leverage ceiling, "highly selective" M&A, deleveraging to 0.7x. This is a balance sheet built either for a large acquisition *or* to be an attractive merger partner — which the EL talks now contextualize.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** — from a steadily-compounding, founder-run standalone beauty platform into a transformational-M&A situation (Estée Lauder combination), executed in lockstep with a CEO succession. The *underlying operating engine* is STABLE-to-STRENGTHENING (FCF inflecting, margins expanding, eight-for-eight market outperformance, balance sheet pristine); but the *equity thesis* has been re-defined by a live, dated corporate event that now dominates everything else.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *Resolve the share-count / valuation basis before trusting the 332% margin-of-safety.* The deep-value tag rests on a €64 Buffett fair value vs €16 price, but the file contains an unreconciled dual-class EPS contradiction (€1.04 adjusted vs 1.99 TTM diluted) and a distorted Altman-Z (2.0) that both flow from the dual-class structure. If the €64 anchor assumes the free-float denominator while the price reflects the full base (or vice-versa), a large slice of the "332%" is a measurement artifact, not real upside. **Net of that caveat, this is a high-quality compounder trading cheap *with* a hard catalyst attached — but the catalyst is a "potential combination" with binary terms-risk, not a self-funded buyback or asset sale Puig controls.** Size to the deal's risk/reward (premium vs break vs dilutive stock-merger), not to the headline MOS.

# Puig Brands S.A. (PUIG.MC) — Forensic Interrogator Dossier
*Prepared by the Interrogator, Investment Committee — capital-at-risk review. Transcripts: H1-2024 (first call as a public company, 2024-09-09) through Q1-2026 (2026-05-01). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2025-12-31.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Puig came public in May 2024 with a tight, repeatable story and has told essentially the same story every quarter since — which is itself the most important fact about this name. The core pitch from the very first call (2024-09-09): a **"portfolio of Love Brands"** spanning three segments (fragrance & fashion, makeup, skincare), where **"our own brands represent 96% of the net revenues"** (i.e., low licence risk), anchored by three top-10 global prestige fragrance brands (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), guided to **"high single-digit like-for-like growth"** medium-term and a beauty market the company insists **"will still grow at 6%, 7%"** (2024-10-29) even as peers cut their market view.

The arc is one of **consistent over-delivery against a deliberately conservative frame, then a single transformational event that overwhelmed the operating story**:
- **2024 (H1 → Q3 → FY):** sequential *acceleration* — H1 LFL +8.5%, 9M +9.6%, FY +10.9%, with Q4 +14.1% — every quarter beat the prior. Fragrance was the engine (FY24 segment +13.6%); makeup was the persistent sore spot (FY24 −1.3%, dragged by Christian Louboutin/China, Charlotte Tilbury destock, and the Airbrush Flawless setting-spray voluntary withdrawal).
- **2025 (FY, reported 2026-02-18):** the story *broadened and de-risked* — **makeup flipped from the problem child to the fastest grower (+13.7% LFL)**, niche fragrance (Byredo) and APAC (+21.7% LFL) became the new growth legs, EBITDA margin expanded to 20.7%, leverage fell to 0.7x. FY25 LFL +7.8% landed at the *top* of the 6–8% guide. A textbook "operationally everything is working" year.
- **Q1-2026 (2026-05-01):** two regime changes at once. (1) A **new CEO, Jose Manuel Muniesa, delivered his first call** — Marc Puig has stepped from CEO to Chairman. (2) The call opened not with results but with confirmation of **"ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with The Estée Lauder"** (first disclosed 2026-03-23). Operating growth visibly *decelerated* (group LFL +4.7%, fragrance +3.9%) into "market moderation," a −4% FX drag, and a Middle East/travel-retail hit (−1.2% to group, March down ~50% in the region). The Capital Markets Day (April 14) was **postponed on account of the deal talks**.

The throughline that was *introduced and then became the whole thesis*: M&A optionality. Management always reserved the right to be acquisitive ("M&A has been an integral part of our growth," 2026-02-18) — but by Q1-2026 the company had itself become the *target*, not the acquirer. **That is the load-bearing fact for any capital decision today, and as of this writing it has already resolved (see §6/§8).**

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "High single-digit like-for-like growth" medium-term guide | IPO / 2024-09-09 | FY24 LFL **+10.9%**, FY25 LFL **+7.8%** (reported +5.3% after −2.6% FX) | **Delivered / over-delivered.** Two-for-two above or at the top of the frame. Reported growth, however, is materially lower than LFL because FX is a real, persistent drag — the scan's "+5.3% YoY" is the *reported* FY25 number, and it understates the underlying engine. |

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING through FY25, then ABRUPTLY GUARDED in Q1-2026 (CEO transition + live deal).**

- **2024-09-09 (Rising, measured):** confident but careful — *"we're pleased to report a robust first half,"* repeatedly hedging makeup (*"early days,"* *"we'll not rush"*). Defensive only on makeup/China.
- **2024-10-29 (Rising):** *"pleased to report an acceleration in Q3 versus first half,"* retailers *"quite bullish on the fragrance category."* Offensive language dominates.
- **2025-01-30 / 2026-02-18 (Peak confidence):** *"2025 was another year of strong delivery,"* *"comfortably above our guidance,"* *"impressive improvement."* Marc Puig is expansive on Jean Paul Gaultier's Gen-Z capture, candid about Rabanne being *"softer"* — the candor itself reads as confidence (a weak operator hides soft brands).
- **2026-05-01 (Guarded / transitional):** the tone shifts hard. New CEO Muniesa opens with a *scripted, lawyered* deal statement (*"unless an agreement is reached, there can be no assurance… we cannot comment further"*), then a Q1 that is *"a solid start"* but visibly decelerating, with new defensive vocabulary: *"market moderation,"* *"more moderate consumer environment,"* *"softer trend in prestige fragrances,"* a Middle East *"minus 1.2% impact,"* and a *"softer Q1… confident on our overall year."* The shift from offensive (share-gain, launches) to defensive (FX, macro, cost discipline, *"EBITDA margins will remain stable"*) is unmistakable.

The CEO handoff matters for tone-reading: Muniesa is competent and on-message, but the founder's storytelling swagger (the Gaultier/Gen-Z narrative, the 20-year share-gain arc) is gone, replaced by an operator's "disciplined execution" register — appropriate while a transformational deal is live, but it removes a source of narrative premium.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest part of the file.

- IPO guide (high-single-digit LFL) → **FY24 +10.9%** (beat) → **FY25 +7.8%** (top of the explicitly-raised 6–8% range). Two clean years of meeting or beating the number they set.
- They *pre-warned* every soft spot before it hit: makeup sell-in/sell-out gap (flagged H1-24, narrowed Q3-24 exactly as promised); the Ulta pipeline tough comp ("not insignificant… particularly Makeup and skincare in Q4"); the Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal (disclosed, quantified mid-single-digit, then "managed… reshipping"); the Argentina hyperinflation swing (quantified to **€36M**, 2024-10-29). When they say a comp will be tough, it is; when they say a gap will close, it does.
- FY25 EBITDA margin "comfortably above guidance" and explicitly Q1-26 framed *down* ("softer Q1") — i.e., they sandbag the near term and then clear it.

There is a **mild sandbagging pattern** (the 6–8% guide they then top), which on the scan shows as a 67% beat rate (2/3 quarters). For a name where the *operator's word is the asset*, HIGH reliability is well-earned. The only credibility shadow is structural, not managerial: the new CEO has one call of track record, and the company just spent two months in a deal process it could not deliver.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Recurring analyst worries, and how management handled them:
1. **Makeup / Charlotte Tilbury sell-in vs sell-out** (every call, H1-24 onward): the single most-pushed topic. Management was consistently transparent and ultimately *right* (the gap closed; makeup became the FY25 star). But Q1-26 flags it *again* — *"this balance between sell-in and sell-out that will happen in the second quarter"* on the Amazon anniversary — so the issue is recurring, not retired.
2. **Market share "loss" 11.5%→11.1% (FY25):** Tom (Q4-25 call) pushed directly. Management's answer — *"we have lost a little less than what this 0.4% gap shows… very aggressive promotional activity from some of our peers… which we didn't necessarily follow"* — is a *plausible but defensive* non-denial. First time in the file the 20-year share-gain story shows a crack.
3. **Rabanne losing momentum** (David/CIC, FY25): management conceded *"Rabanne has been softer,"* leaning on portfolio diversification. Honest, but the largest brand decelerating is a real watch-item.
4. **China/APAC under-exposure:** framed as opportunity (*"the company that has less penetration in APAC"*), and FY25/Q1-26 APAC +21.7%/+26.1% vindicated the bull read — though management flagged the FY25 APAC number was *inflated by subsidiary consolidation* "we do not expect to repeat," a quiet quality-of-growth caveat.
5. **The Estée Lauder deal (Q1-26):** every analyst wanted detail; management stonewalled by design. The *postponed Capital Markets Day* is the loud tell — they pulled their own flagship investor event because the deal made forward guidance impossible.

Pattern: management does **not** evade operational questions (rare and creditable). The only true non-answers are legally-mandated (the deal) and the one defensive share-loss explanation.

🔴 **Red:**

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary shift Q1-26:** "market moderation," "more moderate consumer environment," "normalized from previously elevated levels," "softer trend in prestige" — this is the language of a *category coming off a post-COVID fragrance boom*, not of a structural grower. Management euphemizes it, but the fragrance super-cycle is visibly cooling, and Puig is most exposed to it (74% of Q1-26 sales).
- **The postponed Capital Markets Day** (April 14 → indefinite) is the clearest non-verbal signal in the file: you do not pull your first investor-day-as-public-company unless something binding is in motion.
- **"We will stay active on M&A" (Marc Puig, FY25)** reads very differently in hindsight — within ~5 weeks of that call the company was confirmed in talks to be *acquired by* Estée Lauder. The acquirer-framing was, at minimum, incomplete.
- **Charlotte Tilbury ownership mechanics:** Puig "will progressively assume full ownership until the beginning of 2031" (FY24 call) — and Tilbury's residual stake + change-of-control rights became a named deal-breaker. The crown-jewel brand carries embedded minority-holder friction.
- **New CFO too:** Q1-26 introduces Miquel Angel Serra as CFO (Joan Ramis/Albiol had been CFO across prior calls). A *simultaneous* CEO and CFO change is a fuller management reset than a routine succession — worth confirming the bench is intact.
- **APAC growth quality caveat:** management volunteered that FY25 APAC was lifted by subsidiary consolidation "we do not expect to repeat" — a subtle admission that the headline +21.7% overstates organic APAC momentum.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (operationally STRENGTHENING) business with a PIVOTING / RESET corporate-event situation.**

Puig is, on the evidence, a genuinely high-quality, well-managed prestige-beauty compounder with elite guidance credibility, real margin expansion, a clean balance sheet (~0.7x), and strong FCF (€1.28B TTM). The operating trajectory is *strengthening* (makeup fixed, niche/APAC added). If this were purely an operating-quality question, it would screen as a buy-the-quality name.

But the file's defining fact is a corporate event, and **the director must weigh one thing above all else:**

> **The load-bearing catalyst is dead.** The Estée Lauder business combination — the only event in this file capable of a step-change re-rate — was **terminated on 2026-05-21**, ~2.5 weeks before this review, on a valuation gap, controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause. The stock fell ~14% on the break and trades ~€16.3 today, *below* its 50-day average — i.e., the market is still digesting the disappointment, and the price already reflects the *absence* of a takeout. There is no live deal premium to capture; what remains is a standalone, decelerating (LFL +10.9%→+7.8%→+4.7%) compounder under a brand-new CEO/CFO, priced at ~11x P/FCF — reasonable but not a screaming bargain once the corrupt 332%-MoS model is discarded.

Before sizing, the director must decide whether they are underwriting **(a)** a *standalone quality compounder* at a fair-to-modestly-cheap multiple with a cooling fragrance cycle and an unproven new management team (a defensible 2–3% position on quality), or **(b)** a *renewed M&A target* betting Estée Lauder or another suitor returns — a thesis that just failed once on structural (not price-only) obstacles (Tilbury clause, dual-family control) and should be treated as SOFT optionality, not a hard catalyst. The scan's catalyst score of 0.5 and the dead deal both argue against (b). **Do not size this on the takeout; size it, if at all, on the standalone franchise — and haircut for decelerating growth, the management reset, and a fragrance super-cycle that management itself now calls "moderated."**

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE

bull_thesis: Puig is a high-quality, founder-built prestige-beauty company that the market is now treating as a failed-deal stock rather than the franchise it really is. There are three pillars to the optimistic case. First, the management team has a strong track record: in its two years as a public company, it met or beat every target it set for itself (it guided at its 2024 listing to high-single-digit like-for-like sales growth, meaning growth excluding the effects of acquisitions and currency swings, and then delivered +10.9% in 2024 and +7.8% in 2025, at the top of a raised 6-8% range), and it warned investors about every soft patch before it arrived (the gap between shipping product to retailers and selling it through to consumers in makeup was flagged in the first half of 2024 and closed exactly as promised; the Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal was disclosed, sized, and managed; the Argentina hyperinflation swing was quantified at 36 million euros). An independent review of the calls rates management's credibility 4 out of 5 -- you are buying an operator whose word is the asset. Second, the portfolio fixes its own weak spots and keeps broadening: makeup went from the laggard of 2024 (down 1.3%) to the fastest grower of 2025 (up 13.7% like-for-like, and up 26.5% in the fourth quarter) on the strength of Charlotte Tilbury; the niche-fragrance line Byredo grew double digits every quarter; and Asia-Pacific grew 21.7% in 2025 and 26.1% in the first quarter of 2026, all off small bases (Tilbury is sold in only about a fifth of the footprint it could reach, and Puig is the least Asia-Pacific-exposed of its peers in a region that is roughly 35% of the global category). The company owns 96% of its three top-ten global prestige fragrance brands. Third, it is a clean cash machine: the margin on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization rose from 20.2% to 20.7% even while the company paid down debt to 0.7 times net debt to that earnings measure; trailing-twelve-month free cash flow (the cash left after running and investing in the business) was 1.28 billion euros at 64% conversion; and return on equity was 28.6%. At about 16.3 euros the stock trades at roughly 11 times free cash flow, below its 50-day average and about 14% under its pre-deal level -- meaning the market threw out both the takeover premium and part of the underlying franchise. Valuing each business line separately on luxury-beauty multiples, and cross-checking against enterprise value to that earnings measure, points to 20-27 euros on a standalone basis, so the business with no deal at all supports 25-65% upside, with any residual takeover interest a free option on top. For this case to play out: like-for-like growth needs to settle in the mid-single digits rather than fall outright as the fragrance boom cools; the new chief executive and chief financial officer need to execute as the founder did; the 20.7% margin needs to hold up against tariffs and currency; and the cloud from the broken deal needs to clear over the next two to three quarters.
bear_thesis: Take the dead deal away and this is a decelerating, currency-battered, fairly-priced beauty stock in the middle of a management reset, and the screen's apparent cheapness is an illusion. Five points make the case. First, the only hard catalyst -- the only event capable of forcing a step-change re-rating -- is gone: the planned combination with Estee Lauder was terminated on 2026-05-21 over a valuation gap, a disagreement among the controlling family, and a change-of-control clause tied to Charlotte Tilbury; the stock fell about 14% and has not recovered. The reasons it broke are structural, not just about price (the Tilbury clause plus the family's dual-class voting control are permanent obstacles to any sale), so 'a buyer comes back' is soft, optional upside, not a thesis. Second, growth is rolling over: like-for-like sales went from +10.9% in 2024 to +7.8% in 2025 to +4.7% in the first quarter of 2026, and only +0.8% as reported after a 4% currency drag. Management's own new vocabulary -- market moderation, a more moderate consumer environment, levels normalizing from previously elevated ones, a softer trend in prestige -- describes a deflating post-pandemic fragrance boom, and Puig is the most fragrance-exposed of its peers at 74% of first-quarter-2026 sales. Its share of the value market already slipped from 11.5% to 11.1% in 2025, the first crack in a 20-year run of share gains, and Rabanne, its largest brand, has softened. Third, the quality is thinner than the headline suggests: return on invested capital is 7.0% against a 28.6% return on equity, and the gap is goodwill from acquisitions (Byredo, Tilbury, Sturm). The reported (statutory) gross margin of 70.4% is flagged as contracting, sitting about a third of a standard deviation below its peak, under tariff, currency, and product-mix pressure, even as the company trumpets a record adjusted margin. A fragrance house trading at L'Oreal-like enterprise-value-to-sales multiples but with roughly half L'Oreal's net margin and a 7% return on invested capital does not deserve L'Oreal's multiple, so the optimistic case's sales multiples are unearned. Fourth, the screen's valuation is corrupt: the 332% margin of safety against a 64-euro 'Buffett' fair value extends 28% historical earnings growth out forever, which is absurd for a 5-billion-euro company decelerating to mid-single-digit growth; an honest discounted cash flow value is about 16.2 euros (roughly the current price), and the earnings-power value (what the business is worth assuming no growth) shows no margin of safety at all. The data feed's trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings per share of 1.99 euros is distorted (the company's own adjusted figure is 1.04 euros) and should not anchor a price-to-earnings multiple. Fifth, the management reset comes at the worst possible time: a new chief executive and a new chief financial officer with a single public earnings call between them, having just failed to deliver the biggest event in the company's history and pulled their own first investor day. What would confirm the bearish case: first-half-2026 results (due the week of July 27) showing like-for-like growth below +4% or a full-year downgrade; further gross-margin compression; or no renewed bid and a drift toward the 13-14 euro 52-week low.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market matters here, and the honest answer is that there is no favorable gap for this strategy -- if anything the street is slightly too optimistic. Sell-side consensus is 'Buy' with an average 12-month target around 19.28 euros (10 buy, 6 hold, 1 sell, implying about +30%) and a high of 26.6 euros. Analysts are still anchored on the pre-deal quality-compounder framing and have not fully reset for three things: the deal's structural death (the Tilbury clause plus dual-family control block any re-approach), the slowdown in like-for-like growth to +4.7% alongside management's own 'market moderation' and 'softer prestige' language, and the contraction in the reported gross margin under tariffs and currency. The screen's 332% margin of safety against a 64-euro fair value is the most wrong number in the file -- it projects 28% historical earnings growth forever, when the honest discounted cash flow value is about the current price and the no-growth earnings-power value shows no cushion. The genuine contrarian view here is the bearish one: the market and the screen are treating a decelerating, currency-exposed, single-event-dependent name at 11 times free cash flow as deep value, when margin-disciplined math (a price-to-earnings multiple on the company's own 1.04-euro adjusted earnings, plus a conservative enterprise-value-to-earnings check) puts fair value around 16-20 euros, roughly where it trades. Wall Street is not looking the wrong way in a way that pays us -- the asymmetric mispricing this strategy hunts for simply is not there.
forcing_function: The catalyst -- the event that forces the re-rating -- is the question here, and there is no positive one live: the one that existed, the Estee Lauder combination, already played out and failed on 2026-05-21. The next dated events are earnings and governance milestones, not re-rating triggers: the annual general meeting on 2026-05-29 (now passed); first-half-2026 results the week of 2026-07-27 (the key near-term print, skewed to the downside on the softer first-quarter guide, currency, and the Middle East); and the indefinitely postponed investor day, whose eventual rescheduling would be the clearest sign management has moved past deal limbo and refocused on standalone strategy (a possible mild positive, with no date attached). The only catalyst that could drive a sharp upside surprise would be a renewed or competing takeover approach -- but the same structural blockers (the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause, the dual-family and dual-class control, and the valuation gap Estee Lauder cited) just defeated the most logical buyer, so this is soft, undated, and hangs on a single yes-or-no outcome -- exactly the kind of catalyst the current market backdrop says to set aside rather than bet on.
valley_of_death: The risky window here -- the concrete hazards over the next three to nine months, before any (soft) re-rating -- comes from operations and the calendar, not from the balance sheet. First, first-half-2026 results (the week of 2026-07-27) are the first clean read after the deal broke. Management has already guided to a softer first quarter and flagged a Middle East and travel-retail drag (a 1.2% hit to the group in the first quarter, about 8 million euros in March, roughly 1% for the half) plus a continuing currency headwind (down 3.6% in the fourth quarter, expected to carry into 2026; the first quarter grew only +0.8% as reported). A like-for-like print below +4% or any full-year guidance trim would confirm the bearish case and pressure the multiple. Second, the Charlotte Tilbury makeup unwind tied to lapping last year's Amazon launch, which management itself flagged would happen in the second quarter -- a mechanical air-pocket in makeup growth after 2025's +13.7% (and +26.5% in the fourth quarter). Third, gross-margin compression: the reported margin of 70.4% is already flagged as contracting, and tariffs (more than 95% of product is made in Europe and imported into the United States), glass and other input-cost inflation, and softer Middle East travel-retail all weigh on the 20.7% earnings-margin guide. Fourth, the deflation of the fragrance boom -- the category is normalizing from previously elevated levels, and Puig is 74% fragrance. None of these is a solvency or balance-sheet event (leverage is 0.7 times, free cash flow is 1.28 billion euros, there is no debt-maturity wall and no cash-burn hump), but each is a near-term earnings and sentiment headwind with no hard catalyst to offset it; the path of least resistance into late July is sideways-to-down toward the 13-14 euro low.
positioning_washout: Forced-selling pressure is limited here, though a real residual overhang remains. Puig pays a dividend and is family and dual-class controlled (the Puig family keeps voting control through unlisted Class A shares), so there is no income-holder base or index-removal mechanism to flush out. The relevant selling has already largely happened: the roughly 14% drop on 2026-05-22 was the merger-arbitrage and event-driven base (investors who had bought to bet on the deal) unwinding the now-dead deal. What remains is a slow bleed rather than a sharp flush -- event-driven holders and 'lucky-escape'-relieved holders trimming, plus quality and growth-at-a-reasonable-price funds re-rating the name down as growth decelerates and the new management team is tested. Because the sharp mechanical selling is behind us and what is left is gradual, there is no capitulation low to buy into -- which is itself a reason not to wait for one (waiting for a wash-out is not warranted here) and to simply pass for now. A fresh forced-seller setup would only appear if a renewed bid arrived, then failed again, and re-ran the arbitrage cycle.
moderator_conclusion: Pass for now, with low conviction (2 out of 5). Puig is a genuinely good business wrapped in a dead situation, and this strategy underwrites situations. The franchise is high-quality -- strong guidance credibility (rated 4 out of 5), a portfolio that fixes its own weak spots (makeup went from down 1.3% to up 13.7%), durable niche and Asia-Pacific growth legs, a 20.7% margin on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, 0.7 times leverage, and 1.28 billion euros of free cash flow -- and on generous luxury-beauty sales multiples, valuing each business line separately, it looks worth more than 30 euros. But two disciplines kill the trade. First, the load-bearing catalyst has already fired: the Estee Lauder combination was terminated on 2026-05-21 on structural grounds (the Tilbury change-of-control clause, dual-family control, a valuation gap), the stock fell about 14% and sits below its 50-day average, and a re-approach is soft, single-outcome optionality rather than a hard event -- a catalyst that has already fired is not an asymmetric special situation, it is at best a defensive position with nothing left to arbitrage. Second, on margin-disciplined math the apparent cheapness evaporates: Puig's 11.8% net margin and 7.0% return on invested capital do not earn L'Oreal-class multiples, so the honest base value, summing the parts, is about 20 euros (a roughly 20-25% premium to about 16.3 euros), the discounted cash flow value is about the current price, the no-growth earnings-power value shows no cushion, and the screen's 332% margin of safety against 64 euros is a corrupt artifact of extending 28% growth forever, to be discarded. The broader market backdrop (read on 2026-06-05) seals it: the current environment specifically penalizes catalysts that have already passed or fallen outside their window, and theses that hang on a single shared yes-or-no outcome, while rewarding structural special situations in thinly-covered corners (distressed and deleveraging names ahead of spin-offs ahead of forced sellers). Puig is the opposite -- a large-cap, 17-analyst-covered consumer compounder whose one event already failed, with merger-arbitrage itself the thinnest and most-compressed corner right now and no live deal even present. Risk and reward on the standalone is only about 1.2-to-1 to 1.5-to-1 (roughly +23% to the base value versus -15% to -20% to the 13-14 euro low) and is contingent on a slow re-rating with no catalyst to force it; the next dated event (first-half results, the week of July 27) skews down on the softer-first-quarter, currency, and Middle East guide, plus the Tilbury Amazon-anniversary makeup air-pocket. This is a watch-the-business name, not a deploy-capital one: pass now, and only revisit on either a fresh, credibly-structured bid, or a genuine capitulation into the 13-euro range with stabilized growth and the new management proven over two or more quarters.
sop_breakdown: Valuing each business line separately (the sum-of-the-parts approach), the base case leans on margin-disciplined anchors over the generous sales-multiple version, because Puig's 11.8% net margin and 7.0% return on invested capital do not yet earn L'Oreal-class sales multiples. Applying moderate luxury-beauty multiples by segment (Fragrance & Fashion of 3.6 billion euros at about 3.3 times sales gives 11.9 billion; Makeup of 845 million at about 3.0 times gives 2.5 billion; Skincare of 551 million at about 2.8 times gives 1.5 billion) yields an enterprise value of about 16.0 billion euros; subtracting net debt of 1.05 billion leaves equity of about 14.9 billion, or roughly 26 euros per share on a gross basis. That is then cut by about 20-25% to roughly 20 euros to account for decelerating growth (+10.9% to +7.8% to +4.7%), a contracting reported gross margin, the management reset, and the absence of a live deal. Several cross-checks bracket it: 16 times enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization on 1.045 billion euros gives 27.7 euros (on the optimistic side); 13 times gives 22.2 euros; about 13 times free cash flow on 1.28 billion gives roughly 29 euros gross; and a discipline check -- 18 times the company's own adjusted earnings per share of 1.04 euros -- gives 18.7 euros, pulling the center to about 19-21 euros. Net: a base fair value of about 20 euros, a modest 20-25% premium to the roughly 16.3 euro price, and nowhere near the four-times implied by the screen's corrupt 64-euro / 332% margin-of-safety figure.
risk_reward: On the franchise, the balance of risk and reward is roughly symmetric to modestly favorable, but this is not an asymmetric special situation. The upside to the base sum-of-the-parts value is from 16.28 euros to about 20 euros, or +23% (and the 32-34 euro optimistic version is a quality re-rating tail, not a catalyst-driven one). The downside to a break is the bearish, margin-disciplined value and the 52-week low, which cluster at 13-16 euros -- roughly -2% to -20%, down to the 13.11 euro low, if the second-quarter growth disappoints and no bid returns. So roughly +23% up against -15% to -20% down on the standalone -- about 1.2-to-1 to 1.5-to-1, and contingent on a slow multiple re-rating, with no hard event to force it. The dead deal removes the high-upside leg entirely; what is left is a hold-quality risk and reward, not an entry-grade one.
catalyst_status: FIRED -- The load-bearing catalyst (an Estee Lauder / Puig business combination, first confirmed 2026-03-23) was TERMINATED on 2026-05-21 (Estee Lauder + Puig joint statement / BusinessWire 2026-05-21; CNBC 2026-05-22; SEC 8-K/A). The deal collapsed on a valuation gap (per EL CEO 'right combination of growth, profitability and price'), controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause -- STRUCTURAL obstacles, not price-only. Puig fell ~14% on 2026-05-22 and at EUR 16.28 (FMP live 2026-06-08, below its EUR 17.19 50-day avg) the break is already in the price; the re-rate is spent and reversed. A renewed bid is SOFT/single-binary optionality (the same structural blockers persist), not a hard dated catalyst. The scan 'catalyst score 0.5' corroborates the absence of a live event.
peer_comps_note: The peer file's verdict is 'cheap versus peers' (the true peers being Coty, Interparfums, the luxury-fragrance names L'Oreal and Richemont, Estee Lauder, and Moncler; there is no direct peer inside our own universe). We agree, but with heavy qualification: the relative cheapness (about 11 times free cash flow, a Graham margin of safety of 0.63) is real but limited, and the peer note itself flags Puig's net margin of about 11.8% and return on invested capital of about 7% as below L'Oreal and Estee Lauder quality levels. That is the crux -- Puig screens cheap on sales and free-cash-flow multiples precisely because its margins and capital efficiency are second-tier among the comps, and it carries a discount for being recently listed, family-controlled, and now nursing a busted deal. Coty trading richer despite lower margins is not a clean signal (Coty's mix and turnaround optics are different). A sanity check: the sales-multiple approach that lifts the value above 30 euros implicitly grants Puig L'Oreal-class multiples it does not yet earn; disciplining to the margin tier its peers actually warrant pulls fair value back to about 20 euros. So 'cheap versus peers' supports a hold-with-a-modest-discount read, not an aggressive-entry mispricing.
interrogator_dossier:
# Puig Brands S.A. (PUIG.MC) — A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup
*Prepared as a capital-at-risk review. Earnings calls covered: the first half of 2024 (its first call as a public company, 2024-09-09) through the first quarter of 2026 (2026-05-01). Financials: the screener scan plus a trailing-twelve-month data feed through 2025-12-31.*

---

## 1. The story so far

Puig went public in May 2024 with a tight, repeatable story and has told essentially the same story every quarter since — which is itself the most important fact about this name. The core pitch from the very first call (2024-09-09): a "portfolio of Love Brands" spanning three segments (fragrance & fashion, makeup, skincare), where "our own brands represent 96% of the net revenues" (meaning low licensing risk), anchored by three top-ten global prestige fragrance brands (Rabanne, Carolina Herrera, Jean Paul Gaultier), guided to high-single-digit like-for-like growth — that is, growth excluding the effects of acquisitions and currency — over the medium term, in a beauty market the company insisted "will still grow at 6%, 7%" (2024-10-29) even as peers cut their market view.

The arc is one of consistent over-delivery against a deliberately conservative frame, then a single transformational event that overwhelmed the operating story:
- **2024 (first half, then third quarter, then full year):** steady acceleration — first-half like-for-like +8.5%, nine-month +9.6%, full-year +10.9%, with the fourth quarter +14.1%; every quarter beat the one before. Fragrance was the engine (full-year segment +13.6%); makeup was the persistent sore spot (down 1.3% for the year, dragged by Christian Louboutin and China, a Charlotte Tilbury destock, and the voluntary withdrawal of the Airbrush Flawless setting spray).
- **2025 (full year, reported 2026-02-18):** the story broadened and de-risked — makeup flipped from problem child to fastest grower (up 13.7% like-for-like), niche fragrance (Byredo) and Asia-Pacific (up 21.7%) became the new growth legs, the margin on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expanded to 20.7%, and leverage fell to 0.7 times. Full-year like-for-like of +7.8% landed at the top of the 6-8% guide. A textbook "operationally, everything is working" year.
- **First quarter of 2026 (2026-05-01):** two big changes at once. First, a new chief executive, Jose Manuel Muniesa, delivered his first call — Marc Puig has stepped from chief executive to chairman. Second, the call opened not with results but with confirmation of an "ongoing discussion regarding a potential business combination with The Estée Lauder" (first disclosed 2026-03-23). Operating growth visibly decelerated (group like-for-like +4.7%, fragrance +3.9%) into "market moderation," a 4% currency drag, and a Middle East and travel-retail hit (1.2% off the group, with the region down about 50% in March). The investor day planned for April 14 was postponed because of the deal talks.

The thread that was introduced and then became the whole thesis: the possibility of a deal. Management always reserved the right to be acquisitive ("M&A has been an integral part of our growth," 2026-02-18) — but by the first quarter of 2026 the company had itself become the target, not the acquirer. That is the load-bearing fact for any capital decision today, and as of this writing it has already resolved (see sections 6 and 8).

---

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-single-digit like-for-like growth over the medium term | listing / 2024-09-09 | 2024 like-for-like **+10.9%**, 2025 like-for-like **+7.8%** (+5.3% as reported after a 2.6% currency drag) | **Delivered, even over-delivered.** Two-for-two at or above the frame. Reported growth, though, is materially lower than like-for-like because currency is a real, persistent drag — the scan's "+5.3% year-over-year" is the reported 2025 number, and it understates the underlying engine. |
| Record gross margin, premiumization working | every call | Scan gross margin **70.4% (flagged contracting)**; management cites **75.8% in the first half of 2024, easing to 75.1% for full-year 2025** | **Mostly corroborated, with a wrinkle.** Management's 75% is the adjusted, segment-level figure; the scan's 70.4% is the reported figure and it is flagged as contracting. Both can be true — but the "record gross margin" drumbeat is richer than the reported line, which is drifting down on mix, tariffs, and currency. The margin sits about a third of a standard deviation below its historical peak per the scan. |
| Margin expansion, "comfortably above guidance" | 2026-02-18 | Adjusted margin on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization **20.7%** (versus 20.2% in 2024); net margin **11.8%** | **Corroborated.** The operating leverage is real; an 11.8% net margin and a 15.0% free-cash-flow margin are solid for a recently listed brand house. The 28.6% return on equity is genuinely high; the **7.0% return on invested capital is the tell** — goodwill from the Byredo, Tilbury, and Sturm acquisitions sits heavy on invested capital, so the return-on-capital story is far less impressive than the return-on-equity headline. |
| Deleveraging, balance-sheet strength | 2026-02-18 | Leverage **0.7 times net debt to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization**; scan net debt 828 million; live correction to **net debt of about 1.05 billion euros** | **Corroborated as low-leverage.** Note: the input labels the 1.05 billion euros as net cash, which is internally inconsistent (1.05 billion is positive net debt and matches 0.7 times roughly 1.05 billion of adjusted earnings). Treat Puig as modestly levered (about 0.7 times), not net cash. Either way the balance sheet is clean and not a constraint. |
| Free-cash-flow strength | scan / trailing twelve months | Scan free-cash-flow margin 15.0%, **up 38% year-over-year**; **trailing-twelve-month free cash flow 1.28 billion euros**; cash conversion 64% (management) | **Corroborated and arguably the best line in the file.** Trailing free cash flow of 1.28 billion euros against a roughly 9.2 billion euro listed market value is about a 14% free-cash-flow yield on the listed share class — though see the share-class caveat in section 8 (this overstates the yield on the whole enterprise). |
; "Outperform the premium beauty market… each of the last 8 quarters"; 2026-05-01; Multi-year: revenue 2.59 billion (2021) to 5.04 billion (2025); earnings per share 0.39 to 1.05 euros; **five-year earnings-per-share growth 28.1% a year, book-value-per-share growth 39.4%**; **Corroborated on trajectory.** The compounding is real and consistent. But the rate is decelerating exactly as the story matures — first-quarter-2026 like-for-like of +4.7% is roughly half the 2024 peak of +10.9%. The "outperform the market" claim survives; the "high-growth" claim is softening to "GDP-plus."; 
| Valuation: "Fair Value 64.16 / margin of safety 332%" (scan) | scan | Discounted-cash-flow fair value **16.2** (about the price); Buffett fair value 64.2; earnings-power-value margin of safety **−100%**; Graham +63% | **The 332% margin of safety is a model artifact and should be discarded.** The owner-earnings model extends 28% historical earnings growth out forever — not credible for a maturing 5-billion-euro beauty house decelerating to mid-single-digit growth. The discounted cash flow (16.2, about spot) and the earnings-power value (−100%, meaning no cushion on current earnings power) are the honest anchors. This is not a 332%-undervalued stock; it is roughly fairly-to-modestly-cheaply priced on cash flow (about 11 times free cash flow), with the cheapness driven by the cloud from the broken deal. |

**In short:** where the numbers can be checked, the operating financials genuinely corroborate management's competence and consistency — this is a high-quality, well-run compounder. The divergence is not management versus reality (they deliver); it is the scan model versus reality (the 332% margin of safety and 64-euro fair value are nonsense) and like-for-like versus reported (currency makes the headline growth look weaker than the franchise).

---

## 3. Tone and confidence over time

**The trajectory was rising through 2025, then turned abruptly guarded in the first quarter of 2026 (the leadership change plus a live deal).**

- **2024-09-09 (rising, measured):** confident but careful — "we're pleased to report a robust first half," repeatedly hedging on makeup ("early days," "we'll not rush"). Defensive only on makeup and China.
- **2024-10-29 (rising):** "pleased to report an acceleration in Q3 versus first half," retailers "quite bullish on the fragrance category." Offensive language dominates.
- **2025-01-30 / 2026-02-18 (peak confidence):** "2025 was another year of strong delivery," "comfortably above our guidance," "impressive improvement." Marc Puig is expansive on Jean Paul Gaultier capturing Gen-Z, and candid that Rabanne is "softer" — the candor itself reads as confidence (a weak operator hides soft brands).
- **2026-05-01 (guarded, transitional):** the tone shifts hard. New chief executive Muniesa opens with a scripted, lawyered deal statement ("unless an agreement is reached, there can be no assurance… we cannot comment further"), then a first quarter that is "a solid start" but visibly decelerating, with new defensive vocabulary: "market moderation," "more moderate consumer environment," "softer trend in prestige fragrances," a Middle East "minus 1.2% impact," and a "softer Q1… confident on our overall year." The shift from offensive (share gains, launches) to defensive (currency, macro, cost discipline, "EBITDA margins will remain stable") is unmistakable.

The leadership handoff matters for reading the tone: Muniesa is competent and on-message, but the founder's storytelling swagger (the Gaultier and Gen-Z narrative, the 20-year share-gain arc) is gone, replaced by an operator's "disciplined execution" register — appropriate while a transformational deal is live, but it removes a source of narrative premium.

---

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Reliability: high.** This is the strongest part of the file.

- Listing guidance (high-single-digit like-for-like) became **2024 of +10.9%** (a beat) and then **2025 of +7.8%** (the top of the explicitly raised 6-8% range). Two clean years of meeting or beating the number they set.
- They pre-warned every soft spot before it hit: the makeup sell-in-versus-sell-out gap (flagged in the first half of 2024, narrowed in the third quarter exactly as promised); the Ulta pipeline tough comparison ("not insignificant… particularly Makeup and skincare in Q4"); the Airbrush setting-spray withdrawal (disclosed, sized as mid-single-digit, then "managed… reshipping"); the Argentina hyperinflation swing (quantified at **36 million euros**, 2024-10-29). When they say a comparison will be tough, it is; when they say a gap will close, it does.
- The 2025 margin on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization came in "comfortably above guidance," and the first quarter of 2026 was explicitly framed down ("softer Q1") — that is, they set a low near-term bar and then clear it.

There is a mild under-promise, over-deliver pattern (the 6-8% guide they then top), which shows up in the scan as a 67% beat rate (two of three quarters). For a name where the operator's word is the asset, high reliability is well-earned. The only credibility shadow is structural, not managerial: the new chief executive has one call of track record, and the company just spent two months in a deal process it could not deliver.

---

## 5. Where analysts pressed

Recurring analyst worries, and how management handled them:
1. **Makeup and Charlotte Tilbury — shipping to retailers versus selling through to consumers** (every call from the first half of 2024 onward): the single most-pushed topic. Management was consistently transparent and ultimately right (the gap closed; makeup became the 2025 star). But the first quarter of 2026 flags it again — "this balance between sell-in and sell-out that will happen in the second quarter" on the Amazon anniversary — so the issue is recurring, not retired.
2. **Market-share "loss" from 11.5% to 11.1% (2025):** Tom (on the fourth-quarter-2025 call) pushed directly. Management's answer — "we have lost a little less than what this 0.4% gap shows… very aggressive promotional activity from some of our peers… which we didn't necessarily follow" — is a plausible but defensive non-denial. The first time in the file the 20-year share-gain story shows a crack.
3. **Rabanne losing momentum** (David at CIC, 2025 full year): management conceded "Rabanne has been softer," leaning on portfolio diversification. Honest, but the largest brand decelerating is a real watch-item.
4. **China and Asia-Pacific under-exposure:** framed as opportunity ("the company that has less penetration in APAC"), and 2025 and first-quarter-2026 Asia-Pacific growth of +21.7% and +26.1% vindicated the bullish read — though management flagged that the 2025 figure was inflated by subsidiary consolidation "we do not expect to repeat," a quiet quality-of-growth caveat.
5. **The Estée Lauder deal (first quarter of 2026):** every analyst wanted detail; management stonewalled by design. The postponed investor day is the loud tell — they pulled their flagship event because the deal made forward guidance impossible.

The pattern: management does not evade operational questions (rare and creditable). The only true non-answers are legally mandated (the deal) and the one defensive share-loss explanation.

---

## 6. Warning signs and reassuring signs

Reassuring:
- Two consecutive years of meeting or beating self-set guidance with pre-warned soft spots (see section 4) — elite execution credibility for a two-year public company.
- The makeup turnaround executed exactly as narrated: down 1.3% (2024) to up 13.7% (2025). Proof the portfolio model heals itself.
- Margin expansion (20.2% to 20.7% on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) plus deleveraging to 0.7 times while growing — clean compounding.
- Niche fragrance (Byredo) and Asia-Pacific as durable new growth legs; Charlotte Tilbury "distributed only in about 1/5 of the footprint it could be in" (real runway, not hype).
- Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of 1.28 billion euros at 64% conversion — the cash generation is genuine.

Warning signs:
- **The load-bearing catalyst — the Estée Lauder combination — was terminated on 2026-05-21** (confirmed live; see section 8). The single biggest re-rating driver in the file is spent, and the stock fell about 14% on the break. Any thesis built on deal optionality is dead as of three weeks before this writing.
- **A leadership change mid-stream:** founder Marc Puig moved to chairman; Jose Manuel Muniesa became chief executive with one call of record, during the most consequential corporate event in the company's public life.
- **Growth decelerating sharply:** +10.9% (2024) to +7.8% (2025) to +4.7% (first quarter of 2026) like-for-like. The trend line is down, and reported growth is worse still after a 4% currency drag.
- **Reported gross margin flagged as contracting** (70.4%, about a third of a standard deviation below its peak) under tariff, currency, and mix pressure, even as management trumpets a "record" adjusted margin.
- **A 7.0% return on invested capital** against a 28.6% return on equity — acquisition goodwill (Byredo, Tilbury, Sturm) means capital efficiency is mediocre; the bullish "quality compounder" framing rests on return on equity, not return on invested capital.
- **The Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause** surfaced (in the deal reporting) as a structural complication to any sale of Puig — a permanent overhang on the takeover-premium thesis.
- **The scan valuation is corrupt** (332% margin of safety, 64-euro fair value) — it must not be used to size the position.

---

## 7. Signals beneath the surface

- **The vocabulary shift in the first quarter of 2026:** "market moderation," "more moderate consumer environment," "normalized from previously elevated levels," "softer trend in prestige" — this is the language of a category coming off a post-pandemic fragrance boom, not of a structural grower. Management softens it, but the fragrance boom is visibly cooling, and Puig is most exposed to it (74% of first-quarter-2026 sales).
- **The postponed investor day** (April 14 to indefinite) is the clearest non-verbal signal in the file: you do not pull your first investor day as a public company unless something binding is in motion.
- **"We will stay active on M&A" (Marc Puig, 2025 full year)** reads very differently in hindsight — within about five weeks of that call the company was confirmed in talks to be acquired by Estée Lauder. The acquirer framing was, at minimum, incomplete.
- **Charlotte Tilbury's ownership mechanics:** Puig "will progressively assume full ownership until the beginning of 2031" (2024 call) — and Tilbury's residual stake plus change-of-control rights became a named deal-breaker. The crown-jewel brand carries embedded minority-holder friction.
- **A new chief financial officer too:** the first quarter of 2026 introduces Miquel Angel Serra as chief financial officer (Joan Ramis and Albiol had held the role across prior calls). A simultaneous chief-executive and chief-financial-officer change is a fuller management reset than a routine succession — worth confirming the bench is intact.
- **The Asia-Pacific growth-quality caveat:** management volunteered that the 2025 Asia-Pacific figure was lifted by subsidiary consolidation "we do not expect to repeat" — a subtle admission that the headline +21.7% overstates organic momentum there.

---

## 8. The capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Read: a stable, operationally strengthening business wrapped in a corporate situation that is now resetting.**

Puig is, on the evidence, a genuinely high-quality, well-managed prestige-beauty compounder with strong guidance credibility, real margin expansion, a clean balance sheet (about 0.7 times), and strong free cash flow (1.28 billion euros trailing twelve months). The operating trajectory is strengthening (makeup fixed, niche and Asia-Pacific added). If this were purely an operating-quality question, it would screen as a buy-the-quality name.

But the file's defining fact is a corporate event, and the director must weigh one thing above all else:

> The load-bearing catalyst is dead. The Estée Lauder business combination — the only event in this file capable of a step-change re-rating — was terminated on 2026-05-21, about two and a half weeks before this review, over a valuation gap, a controlling-family disagreement, and the Charlotte Tilbury change-of-control clause. The stock fell about 14% on the break and trades around 16.3 euros today, below its 50-day average — meaning the market is still digesting the disappointment, and the price already reflects the absence of a takeout. There is no live deal premium to capture; what remains is a standalone, decelerating (like-for-like +10.9% to +7.8% to +4.7%) compounder under a brand-new chief executive and chief financial officer, priced at about 11 times free cash flow — reasonable but not a screaming bargain once the corrupt 332%-margin-of-safety model is discarded.

Before sizing, the director must decide whether they are underwriting (a) a standalone quality compounder at a fair-to-modestly-cheap multiple, with a cooling fragrance cycle and an unproven new management team (a defensible 2-3% position on quality), or (b) a renewed takeover target, betting Estée Lauder or another buyer returns — a thesis that just failed once on structural rather than price-only obstacles (the Tilbury clause, dual-family control) and should be treated as soft optionality, not a hard catalyst. The scan's catalyst score of 0.5 and the dead deal both argue against (b). Do not size this on the takeout; size it, if at all, on the standalone franchise — and discount for decelerating growth, the management reset, and a fragrance boom that management itself now calls "moderated."
